requirementCueLines: Suggested First- and Second-Year School Courses: ENGL 131 (or other 5-credit English composition course); BIOL 180, BIOL 200, BIOL 220; one of CHEM 120, CHEM 142, CHEM 143 or CHEM 145; one of CHEM 220, CHEM 152, CHEM 153, CHEM 155 or OCEAN 295; one of Q SCI 291, MATH 124, or MATH 134; one of Q SCI 292, MATH 125, or MATH 135; 5 credits English Composition.
Admission Requirements

Minimum course requirements: one of Q SCI 291, MATH 124, or MATH 134; one of Q SCI 292, MATH 125, or MATH 135; one of CHEM 120, CHEM 142, CHEM 143, or CHEM 145; one of CHEM 220, CHEM 152, CHEM 153, CHEM 155, or OCEAN 295; either BIOL 180 or both BIOL 161 and BIOL 162; 5 credits of English composition.
Minimum grade requirements: Minimum 2.00 cumulative GPA in courses required for admission
Completion Requirements
Written Communication (15 credits):
English Composition (C): one 5 credit course from the University list with a minimum 2.0 grade (ENGL 131 preferred)
Writing (W): 10 credits from the University W course list
Reasoning (RSN) (15 credits):
Calculus (10 credits): one of Q SCI 291, MATH 124, or MATH 134; one of Q SCI 292, MATH 125, or MATH 135
Statistics (5 credits): Q SCI 381 or STAT 311
Diversity (DIV) (5 credits)
Areas of Inquiry
Arts and Humanities (A&H) (10 credits):
one of COM 200, COM 202, COM 220, COM 231, LING 233/ANTH 233/COM 233, COM 234, COM 238, or COM 270
5 credits from the University A&H list
Natural Sciences (NSc) (33-42 credits):
Biology (10-15 credits) : complete one of the following:
BIOL 180, BIOL 200; either BIOL 220 or FISH 270/MARBIO 270/OCEAN 270
BIOL 161 and BIOL 162
Chemistry (10-12 credits): complete one of the following:
CHEM 120; either CHEM 220 or OCEAN 295
CHEM 142; either CHEM 152 or OCEAN 295
CHEM 143 and CHEM 153
CHEM 145 and CHEM 155
Earth Systems (3-5 credits): one of ATMOS 211, ESRM 210, ESS 201, ESS 212, or ESS 230/OCEAN 230
Additional NSc credits (10 credits): 10 credits chosen from the University NSc list (outside the major)
Social Sciences (SSc) (20 credits):
Economics (5 credits): one of ECON 200, ECON 201, ESRM 235/ENVIR 235/ECON 235, or FISH 230/ECON 230
Additional SSc credits (15 credits): 10 credits chosen from the University SSc list (outside the major); 5 credits met by coursework in the major
Major Requirements
67 credits
Core Courses (22 credits): ESRM 200, ESRM 201, ESRM 250, ESRM 300/SBSE 300, ESRM 304.

Electives (45 credits): upper-division courses from within the School of Environmental and Forest Sciences or from a list of approved courses. Refer to department website for list of approved courses. Minimum 25 credits at the 400-level. Maximum 10 credits from ESRM general elective course list. Maximum 10 credits from Q SCI elective course list.
Additional electives as needed to reach 180 credits

Natural Resources and Environmental Management (NREM) is an integrated approach to studying the interaction of physical, biological, and social processes on ecological systems at a wide range of spatial scales. The NREM option focuses on applied aspects of environmental management that create, sustain, and alter landscapes to achieve biological diversity and integrity as well as social purposes.
62-66 credits
Core Courses (22 credits): ESRM 200, ESRM 201, ESRM 250, ESRM 300/SBSE 300, ESRM 304
Natural Resource and Environmental Management Courses (29 credits): ESRM 323, ESRM 331/BIOL 331, ESRM 350; ESRM 381, ESRM 400, ESRM 426, ESRM 470
Electives (11-15 credits): at least one course from each of the categories below. See department website for list of approved courses.
Ecosystem Threats (3-5 credits)
Adaptive Management (5 credits)
Economics and Society (3-5 credits)

Students in the Restoration Ecology and Environmental Horticulture (REEH) option learn and apply fundamental concepts of biology, plant science, and ecology. This disciplinary knowledge, with supporting coursework and experience, allows students to become accomplished in producing plant materials, managing sustainable landscapes, repairing damaged ecosystems, and participating in large interdisciplinary projects.
Economics (5 credits): one of ECON 200, ECON 201, ESRM 235/ENVIR 235/ECON 235, or FISH230/ECON 230
Restoration Ecology and Environmental Horticulture Courses (45 credits):
Electives (35 credits): see department website for list of approved courses
Capstone (10 credits): complete one of the following:
ESRM 462/T BIOL 462, ESRM 463/T BIOL 463, ESRM 464/T BIOL 464
ESRM 494 and ESRM 495
ESRM 494 and ESRM 496
71-77 credits
Sustainable Forest Management Courses (39-40 credits): ESRM 323, ESRM 331/BIOL 331, ESRM 368, ESRM 400, either ESRM 426 or FISH 447/ESRM 447, ESRM 428, ESRM 430, SBSE 461/ESRM 461, ESRM 470
Electives (10-15 credits): at least one course from each of the categories below. See department website for list of approved courses.
Forest Ecology and Biology (3-5 credits)
Forest Resource Management (4-5 credits)
Forest Resource Policy, Economics, and Administration (3-5 credits)

Wildlife Conservation is the science and art of managing animal populations and their related resources. This option offers coursework in wildlife ecology, quantitative science, and the social and political aspects of wildlife conservation issues. Students can expect hands-on field experiences including how to identify, capture, and handle animals; and how to assess, map, and plan wildlife habitats. Instruction on writing technical reports and scientific papers, and presentation of findings and implementation of wildlife conservation plans will be covered.​
68 credits
Core Courses (22 credits): ESRM 200, ESRM 201, ESRM 250, ESRM 300, ESRM 304
Wildlife Conservation Courses (36 credits): ESRM 350, ESRM 351, ESRM 441, ESRM 450, ESRM 451/Q SCI 451, ESRM 458; Q SCI 482; one of ESRM 452, ESRM 453, or ESRM 459
ESRM 462/T BIOL 462, ESRM 463/T BIOL 463, T BIOL 464/ESRM 464
